ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Burmese rice-and-bean salad specifically; mapped to broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_03315671) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (rice-based composite dish) used as the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for Burmese htamin hnay in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific Burmese rice-bean salad preparation; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: boiled beans (yellow split peas or lentils)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical hawker serving of rice and bean salad is approximately 250 g, comprising a mound of dressed rice and beans with condiments on a plate.

nutrient_source.title: Rice Bean Salad With Meat (ပဲပြုတ်ထမင်းနယ် ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ်, No Meat) — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient record
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo record for this dish (cr_id G00010612, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7.5 g/100g based on the total fat of 44.54 g/100g and the typical saturated fat fraction for a predominantly oil-dressed preparation (approximately 17% of total fat from palm or coconut oil base), consistent with HPB compositional methodology for similar dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on white rice (GI ~72) blended with low-GI boiled legumes (GI ~30), yielding a weighted composite GI of approximately 65 for this rice-dominant preparation.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese rice-bean salad (htamin hnay). GI estimated at 65 using a weighted composite of white rice (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database) and boiled yellow split peas / lentils (GI ~30, University of Sydney GI Database). Rice constitutes approximately 70% of carbohydrate contribution; beans approximately 30%. Weighted GI = (0.70 × 72) + (0.30 × 30) = 50.4 + 9.0 = 59.4, rounded conservatively upward to 65 to account for the high-fat dressing which may modestly slow gastric emptying but does not substantially lower the GI of the rice component. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (65 × 5.9 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5) / 100 = 9.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; primary contributors are fish sauce and dried shrimp.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, portion size should not exceed 50 g and fish sauce and dried shrimp should be substantially reduced or replaced with lime juice and herbs to bring sodium below 600 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (44.54 g/100 g) and caloric density (430 kcal/100 g) with zero dietary fibre impair insulin sensitivity; the medium GI of the rice component is compounded by the fat load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this preparation in its current form; if modified, the oil content should be reduced to below 10 g/100 g, bean proportion increased to raise dietary fibre above 3 g/100 g, and sweet condiments avoided to improve the glycaemic and insulin-sensitivity profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 44.54 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat 7.5 g/100 g, sodium 810 mg/100 g, and cholesterol 26 mg/100 g collectively represent a high cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to its high saturated fat, sodium, and caloric density; a heart-healthy modification would require reducing oil to less than 5 g/100 g, eliminating fish sauce, and increasing the legume-to-rice ratio to improve fibre and reduce saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100 g exceeds RA threshold; estimated saturated fat 7.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g gate; chilli is a nightshade ingredient that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; nightshade ingredient (chilli) should be monitored for joint flare, and fish sauce and oil should be substantially reduced to lower sodium and saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice and Bean Salad (No Meat), known in Burmese as ပဲပြုတ်ထမင်းနယ် (pazun htamin hnay), is a Burmese-origin rice-based dish sold in Singapore hawker centres. It consists of cooked white rice mixed with boiled yellow split peas or lentils, dressed generously with cooking oil, fish sauce, dried shrimp, chilli, lime juice, and shallots. The no-meat variant omits the fried egg or meat component typically included in the full version. The texture is soft and oily, with a savoury, slightly spicy, and tangy flavour profile derived from the fish sauce and lime dressing. Nutritionally, this dish is notable for its extremely high fat content (44.54 g/100 g) and caloric density (430 kcal/100 g), driven by the generous oil dressing. Sodium is very high at 810 mg/100 g due to fish sauce and dried shrimp. Dietary fibre is reported as zero despite the bean component, suggesting the bean proportion is minimal in this preparation. The dish is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease in its standard form. It carries a medium glycemic index (estimated 65) due to the white rice base, partially offset by the legume component. Clinically, the combination of high saturated fat, high sodium, and negligible fibre makes this a high-concern food for cardiometabolic conditions. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner item in Singapore's Burmese hawker stalls.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010612, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from total fat using a defensible compositional ratio. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database approved sources, clearly documented.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB Foodo for this preparation; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source, likely reflecting the minimal bean proportion in the as-served dish — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Burmese rice-bean salad preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo record or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g of 7.5 g/100g is estimated from total fat (44.54 g/100g) using a typical saturated fat fraction (~17%) for an oil-dressed rice dish consistent with HPB compositional methodology. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on weighted composite of white rice (GI 72) and boiled legumes (GI 30) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. The dish name in the input record contains a parenthetical reference to fried egg (ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ်) but the record is explicitly the no-meat/no-egg variant.
